message.content:
Synthesize these self-refutation-surviving findings for hc-assay (4 passes hardened — expect few/low-severity, this is a convergence check). Deduplicate, rank by severity, assign IDs H-001.., record dimension, drop already-fixed/speculative.

[
  {
    "id": "PT-1",
    "severity": "high",
    "file": "tests/test_immutability.py",
    "line": 47,
    "title": "G-004 in-process flaky guard: unfreeze(frozenset) order test mis-passes ~30% of runs against the buggy code",
    "description": "test_unfreeze_frozenset_of_unorderable_types_is_deterministic (lines 43-48) asserts unfreeze(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) == [1,2,'a','b'] while running at the AMBIENT PYTHONHASHSEED (pytest does not pin it). The correct implementation sorts (src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:158-161), so the test passes today. But its stated job is to be a regression guard against a revert to raw set iteration (e.g. `list(value)`). As such a guard it is probabilistic: I enumerated 200 seeds and raw frozenset iteration of {1,'a',2,'b'} equals the stable order [1,2,'a','b'] in 60/200 = 30% of seeds. So on the buggy raw-iteration code this test would still PASS ~30% of the time, depending only on the process hash seed. The test's own comment at lines 52-53 admits 'the in-process assertion above can pass ~1/3 of the time even on the buggy raw-iteration code.' Secondary defect: lines 47 and 48 use {1,'a',2,'b'} and {'b',2,'a',1}, which are the SAME set object — identical iteration order in any given process — so line 48 adds zero additional coverage and gives a false impression of testing two independent orderings.",
    "poc": "# enumerate seeds: raw iteration == stable order 30% of the time\nfor s in range(200):\n    out = subprocess.run([sys.executable,'-c',\"print(list(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})))\"],\n                         env={'PYTHONHASHSEED':str(s),'PATH':''},capture_output=True,text=True).stdout.strip()\n    # out == \"[1, 2, 'a', 'b']\" for seeds 4,8,10,12,13,14,18,23,29,33,35,37,39,... (60/200)\n# => if unfreeze() reverts to `return [unfreeze(v) for v in value]`, this test passes whenever\n#    the ambient PYTHONHASHSEED lands on one of those ~30% of seeds, masking the regression.",
    "suggested_fix": "Treat the in-process assertion as documentation only, not a guard; rely on the subprocess test (test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess) for discrimination. If keeping the in-process check, parametrize it to run under several pinned PYTHONHASHSEEDs that are known to differ from the stable order (re-run in subprocess), and replace the duplicate {'b',2,'a',1} set on line 48 with a genuinely different input (e.g. a 3-type mix like frozenset({1,'a',2.5,'b'})) so the two assertions actually cover distinct cases."
  },
  {
    "id": "X-1",
    "severity": "medium",
    "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py",
    "line": 414,
    "title": "HIGH_STAKES tier lacks the output-length cap that BULK tier enforces (asymmetric runaway-generation guard)",
    "description": "The two reasoning tiers are parallel mechanisms that both must bound a single LLM call's cost/duration. BULK got a hard generation cap: `_bulk_complete` always sets `num_predict=BULK_NUM_PREDICT` (line 99, 368), and the docstring at lines 95-99 explicitly argues this is 'the real guard' because a wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a backend call already blocked in generation. HIGH_STAKES (`_high_stakes_complete`, lines 414-503) builds `ClaudeAgentOptions` with NO max-output-tokens equivalent — there is no `max_tokens`/output bound passed at all. The result is asymmetric: a runaway BULK generation is bounded by tokens AND wall-clock, but a runaway HIGH_STAKES generation is bounded only by `anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` wall-clock. For the tier explicitly described as 'gated, traced' and the one that consumes the fixed-cost subscription budget, the cheaper/safer of the two guards (a bounded output) was applied only to the local tier. The same reasoning the BULK docstring uses ('the wall-clock timeout cannot interrupt a call already blocked in the backend') applies to the subscription subprocess.",
    "poc": "Set ASSAY_BULK_NUM_PREDICT=2048 (default). A BULK call cannot emit more than 2048 tokens. The parallel HIGH_STAKES call has no analogous bound: a prompt that elicits a very long completion runs until HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT (300s default), consuming the full subscription turn and 300s of a bounded-pool worker slot, where BULK would have stopped at 2048 tokens well inside its window. grep for `num_predict` shows it only in `_bulk_complete`; grep for `max_tokens` in seam.py returns nothing.",
    "suggested_fix": "Add a parallel HIGH_STAKES output cap (e.g. ASSAY_HIGH_STAKES_MAX_TOKENS) and pass it to ClaudeAgentOptions (or the SDK's max-output-tokens option), mirroring `num_predict`. If the Agent SDK genuinely cannot bound output, document that asymmetry explicitly in the HIGH_STAKES docstring the way BULK's is documented, so the gap is intentional-and-recorded rather than silent."
  },
  {
    "id": "X-2",
    "severity": "medium",
    "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py",
    "line": 69,
    "title": "discover_and_confirm lacks the on_step provenance hook that adjudicate_with_baseline has (asymmetric audit-trail wiring)",
    "description": "`adjudicate` / `adjudicate_with_baseline` and `discover_and_confirm` are the two parallel firewall runners (the discovery module docstring even calls itself 'The mirror of assay_engine.methodology.adjudication'). They were hardened asymmetrically for per-step provenance. `adjudicate_with_baseline` takes an `on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None` parameter (adjudication.py:193, 264-267) so a caller (e.g. the pipeline at pipeline.py:641-668) can record each pre-registered hypothesis and its verdict to the hash-chained provenance trail *as each happens* (#93 in the code comment). `discover_and_confirm` (discovery.py:69-165) has NO such hook: a study driving the standalone discovery runner cannot capture per-hypothesis/per-verdict provenance in order; it gets only the returned list, forcing a post-hoc batch record that loses the 'recorded before the next step runs' GOVERNANCE §3 ordering guarantee that the adjudication path preserves. One mirror got the governance seam; the other did not.",
    "poc": "Call adjudicate_with_baseline(..., on_step=lambda h,v: trail.record(...)) — each verdict lands in the trail in confirmation order. There is no equivalent for discover_and_confirm(corpus, split, discover=..., confirm=..., authority=...): the function signature accepts no on_step, so a standalone discovery study can only record verdicts after the whole loop returns (or by re-implementing the loop), defeating the append-before-next-step ordering the adjudication twin enforces.",
    "suggested_fix": "Add an `on_step: Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None = None` parameter to discover_and_confirm and invoke it after each verdict is validated (right after the misattribution check at discovery.py:164), mirroring adjudication.py:264-267, so both firewall runners expose the same per-step provenance seam."
  },
  {
    "id": "X-3",
    "severity": "low",
    "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py",
    "line": 150,
    "title": "Confirm step double-dip leak check (assert_confirm_only) enforced by confirm_unit_level but not by the discovery runners",
    "description": "`DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only` (firewalls.py:126-145) is the runtime guard that the confirm step touched ONLY held-out ids (not empty, no discovery-id leak, none outside the confirm partition). `confirm_unit_level` calls it on the caller-supplied `evaluated_ids` (confirm.py:188) — the standalone confirm primitive verifies which ids were actually evaluated. The two composing runners that ALSO confirm — `discover_and_confirm` (discovery.py) and the pipeline's DISCOVERY/CONFIRM block (pipeline.py:558-576) — never call `assert_confirm_only`. They guard only that the held-out *partition* is non-empty (discovery.py:120, pipeline.py:526) and subset the corpus to confirm_ids, but the study-supplied `confirm`/`confirm_held_out` callable is handed the held-out corpus and is trusted to evaluate within it: nothing checks the ids it actually scored. So a confirmer that internally pulls in discovery-partition units (e.g. via attributes/metadata references the subset_corpus docstring admits it cannot police) is caught by the standalone primitive but NOT by either runner. The same Firewall-B leak check exists on one parallel mechanism and is absent on the other two.",
    "poc": "Build a confirm_held_out that ignores its held_out arg and evaluates discovery-partition units. confirm_unit_level(hyp, split=split, evaluated_ids=<discovery ids>, ...) raises FirewallViolation 'touched N discovery id(s) — double-dipping'. The same logical leak through discover_and_confirm(... confirm=that_callable ...) or run_study completes cleanly because neither runner asks the confirmer which ids it evaluated, so assert_confirm_only is never exercised.",
    "suggested_fix": "Have the discovery runners obtain (or require) the set of ids the confirmer evaluated and pass it through split.assert_confirm_only before accepting the verdict — e.g. change HeldOutConfirmer to return evaluated ids alongside the verdict, or have the runner pass split to the confirmer and assert. At minimum, document in both runners that the confirm-time double-dip check is delegated to the study (as adjudication documents its frame-isolation caveat), so the asymmetry with confirm_unit_level is explicit."
  },
  {
    "id": "X-4",
    "severity": "low",
    "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py",
    "line": 180,
    "title": "Direction shape-validation present in confirm_unit_level but the keyed-direction resolution differs from confirm_whole_corpus (asymmetric tail-binding)",
    "description": "confirm_whole_corpus and confirm_unit_level are the two parallel confirmatory mechanisms. They handle the pre-registered `predicted_direction` asymmetrically in a way that is documented but worth flagging as a cross-tier divergence in guard strength. confirm_whole_corpus routes the locked direction through `_resolve_direction` (confirm.py:254-289), which REFUSES a directionless-but-locked hypothesis (None → HARKing guard, #G-001) and rejects any tail not in {greater,less} that would silently flip the verdict (#128). confirm_unit_level (confirm.py:180-186) only *shape-validates* a non-None predicted_direction and otherwise lets the caller-supplied `direction_supports_claim` flag decide — a locked hypothesis with predicted_direction=None passes unit-level confirmation with the tail effectively chosen post-hoc by the caller, exactly the HARKing case _resolve_direction refuses on the whole-corpus side. The asymmetry is intentional (documented at confirm.py:163-169) but the unit-level path lacks the directionless-lock refusal its twin enforces, so a study can pick the supporting/contradicting polarity after seeing held-out data on the unit-level path while the whole-corpus path forbids it.",
    "poc": "Lock a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis with predicted_direction=None. confirm_unit_level(..., direction_supports_claim=True) returns 'supported'; flip the flag and the SAME locked hypothesis returns 'contradicted'. The whole-corpus twin refuses this outright: _resolve_direction raises 'predicted_direction was not pre-registered ... refused (#G-001)'. The polarity is caller-chosen at confirm time on one mechanism and locked-or-refused on the other.",
    "suggested_fix": "If unit-level studies are expected to bind the tail at lock time, add a symmetric refusal: when a unit-level hypothesis is locked, require predicted_direction to be non-None and (optionally) require the study to derive direction_supports_claim from it. If the asymmetry must stand, strengthen the confirm_unit_level docstring's caveat into an explicit runtime warning when direction_supports_claim is supplied for a directionless-locked hypothesis, mirroring _gate_preregistration's authority=None warning."
  },
  {
    "id": "X-5",
    "severity": "low",
    "file": "src/assay_engine/orchestration/gatenode.py",
    "line": 114,
    "title": "Gate-decision provenance recording: graph gate node warns-but-allows a missing recorder while run_study's gate path always records (asymmetric audit-trail guarantee)",
    "description": "There are two parallel governance-gate execution paths and they were hardened asymmetrically with respect to the hash-chained provenance trail. In run_study, the gate handler ALWAYS records to the trail before enforcing (pipeline.py:326-342, run_gate): every gate decision is unconditionally appended to the tamper-evident chain. The LangGraph graph path (make_gate_node, gatenode.py:90-161) makes the trail recorder OPTIONAL: with recorder=None it merely emits a warning (lines 114-120) and records the decision only into (non-tamper-evident) LangGraph state. The docstring itself (lines 109-112) calls this 'a GOVERNANCE §3 audit gap' where a checkpoint edit flipping rejected→approved is undetectable by verify_records. So the same governance concern (gate decisions must be in the hash-chained trail) is structurally guaranteed on the run_study path and merely warned-about on the graph path. The two parallel mechanisms enforce GOVERNANCE §3 at different strengths.",
    "poc": "make_gate_node(gate, propose) with no recorder builds successfully (only a warning). Operator approvals/rejections then live solely in LangGraph checkpoint state; verify_records on the provenance trail cannot detect a checkpoint tamper that flips the decision. The equivalent run_study path has no such opt-out — run_gate always calls trail.record before raising/continuing, so every decision is in the chain.",
    "suggested_fix": "Make the recorder mandatory on make_gate_node (raise instead of warn when recorder is None), matching run_study's unconditional trail.record — or, if a recorder-less graph node must remain supported, document and gate it behind an explicit opt-out flag (e.g. allow_unrecorded=True) so shipping the audit-gap configuration is a conscious choice rather than the default, mirroring how run_study forces gate_handler to be passed explicitly."
  },
  {
    "id": "M-1",
    "severity": "high",
    "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py",
    "line": "101-143, 292-397",
    "title": "alpha and stability_threshold are post-hoc free parameters, not pre-registered — HARKing on the decision threshold survives the firewall",
    "description": "The pre-registration digest (canonical_hypothesis_digest, preregistration.py:109-119) cryptographically binds statement/kind/origin/test_name/decision_rule/source_claim_id/predicted_direction/params, and audit pass 4 (#G-001) closed direction-after-the-fact HARKing. But the two parameters that actually decide the verdict — the significance level `alpha` and the `stability_threshold` — are NOT fields on Hypothesis, are NOT in the digest, and are supplied as plain runtime arguments to verdict_from_pvalue / confirm_whole_corpus / confirm_unit_level at confirmation time. Nothing in the engine reads `hypothesis.params` or `hypothesis.decision_rule` to constrain the confirm-time alpha/threshold. `verdict_from_pvalue` even *constructs* the recorded decision_rule string from the runtime alpha (`rule = f\"...alpha={alpha}...\"`, line 131) instead of comparing it against the locked `decision_rule`. So an analyst (or a study confirmer) who has already seen the baseline/null distribution can choose alpha=0.05 vs 0.10, or stability_threshold=0.9 vs 0.6, to flip indeterminate↔supported — exactly the post-hoc-threshold HARKing the pre-registration firewall claims to prevent. The locked `decision_rule` text (which a study might write 'alpha=0.01' into) is decorative: the engine never enforces it. This defeats the methodological guarantee that 'the decision rule is committed before confirmation' for its most decision-bearing numbers.",
    "poc": "1) Build/lock a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis with predicted_direction='greater' (alpha is NOT a field, so nothing about the threshold is locked). 2) After confirmation data is in hand, observe the null distribution and the observed statistic. 3) confirm_whole_corpus(h, observed=X, null_distribution=null, alpha=0.05, resample_statistics=R, stability_threshold=0.9, authority=auth) → indeterminate ('does not beat null at alpha'). 4) Re-run the SAME locked hypothesis (require_preregistered still passes — content unchanged) with alpha=0.10, stability_threshold=0.6 → supported. The verdict was changed purely by post-hoc threshold choice; require_preregistered raised nothing because alpha/stability_threshold are outside the digest. Same applies to confirm_unit_level's `alpha`.",
    "suggested_fix": "Make alpha and stability_threshold (and the test sidedness already covered) part of the pre-registered, content-bound contract. Concretely: add `alpha` (and optional `stability_threshold`) as typed fields on Hypothesis, include them in canonical_hypothesis_digest's payload, and have confirm_whole_corpus/confirm_unit_level READ them from the locked hypothesis instead of accepting them as caller arguments (or, if kept as arguments, require they equal the locked values and raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch — mirroring _resolve_direction's cross-check). Until then the runners (adjudication/discovery/pipeline) cannot certify the threshold was committed before the data was seen."
  },
  {
    "id": "M-2",
    "severity": "medium",
    "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py",
    "line": "96-126, 137-143",
    "title": "`powered` is a trusted, unbound caller flag that gates indeterminate↔decisive after the data is seen",
    "description": "verdict_from_pvalue takes `powered: bool` from the caller and routes `not powered → indeterminate` (line 137-138). `powered` is neither a Hypothesis field nor part of the pre-registration digest, and the engine performs no power calculation. A study confirmer therefore decides AFTER seeing the held-out result whether the test was 'powered enough' to be decisive. Combined with M-1, this is a second post-hoc lever: an unfavorable significant-but-against-the-claim result can be neutralized to indeterminate by asserting powered=False, or a favorable result promoted to decisive by asserting powered=True. The docstring frames `powered` as a method-capability fact, but nothing binds it to a pre-stated power/sample-size criterion.",
    "poc": "For one locked unit-level hypothesis with a fixed held-out statistic/p_value, call confirm_unit_level(..., powered=True, ...) → supported/contradicted; call again with powered=False → indeterminate. Both pass the identical pre-registration check; the decisiveness was chosen post-hoc.",
    "suggested_fix": "Require the power/decisiveness criterion (e.g. minimum n, minimum detectable effect) to be pre-registered in the hypothesis (params bound in the digest) and have the engine derive `powered` from the held-out sample it actually evaluated against that locked criterion, rather than accepting an opaque boolean. At minimum, record in evidence the locked power criterion and assert the supplied `powered` is consistent with it."
  },
  {
    "id": "M-3",
    "severity": "medium",
    "file": "src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py",
    "line": "113-119, 146-197",
    "title": "Locked `test_name` is bound in the digest but never enforced against the test actually executed (test-choice HARKing at unit level)",
    "description": "test_name is included in canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:114), so it cannot be swapped after locking — but `grep` shows test_name is read NOWHERE except when building the digest. No confirmer or runner checks that the test the study actually ran matches the pre-registered test_name. On the unit-level path this matters most: confirm_unit_level receives only an opaque `statistic`/`p_value`/`direction_supports_claim` (the engine 'cannot itself infer the tail', lines 163-169), and verdict_from_pvalue records a hard-coded test_description='held-out unit-level test' (line 196) rather than the locked test_name. So a study can pre-register test_name='mann_whitney_u', then, after seeing the held-out data, run whichever test gives the desired p_value and feed it in — the pre-registration check still passes because the content (including the now-meaningless test_name) is unchanged. The methodology docstrings claim the locked content governs the verdict; for the test choice it does not.",
    "poc": "Lock a unit-level hypothesis with test_name='mann_whitney_u'. After seeing the held-out partition, compute p_value with a t-test (which happens to clear alpha) and call confirm_unit_level(h, ..., p_value=that_t_test_p, direction_supports_claim=True, authority=auth) → supported. require_preregistered raises nothing; the executed test silently differs from the locked one.",
    "suggested_fix": "Have the engine-side confirmer pass the test identity it ran and assert it equals hypothesis.test_name (raise PreRegistrationError on mismatch), and propagate test_name into verdict_from_pvalue's test_description so the recorded decision_rule reflects the locked test rather than a generic label. Where the engine cannot see the test (fully study-owned confirmers), document explicitly that test-choice integrity is an un-enforced caller obligation so the docstrings stop over-claiming."
  },
  {
    "id": "SC-1",
    "severity": "low",
    "file": "src/assay_engine/pipeline.py",
    "line": 739,
    "title": "Provenance-artifact temp file leaked on every tracked run (delete=False, never unlinked)",
    "description": "In run_study's best-effort _log_trail callback, the hash-chained provenance trail is serialized to a NamedTemporaryFile opened with delete=False, then handed to tracker.log_artifact(rid, path). The temp file is never unlinked afterward. MLflow's log_artifact copies the file into the artifact store but does not remove the source, so each successful study run that has a tracker+run_id leaves one orphaned <tmp>_provenance.json in the system temp directory. Over a long-running campaign / many studies this accumulates unbounded disk under TMPDIR. The leak also persists if log_artifact raises, because _log_trail runs inside track(), which swallows the exception (recording a tracking_error) but the file is already created and orphaned. This is the one place in the codebase that copies the in-memory provenance (which can contain study summaries / payloads) to a world-readable default-mode temp path and leaves it there — a minor data-at-rest hygiene issue on top of the disk leak. Contrast with versioning.py:74 which correctly uses mkstemp + try/finally tmp.unlink(missing_ok=True).",
    "poc": "Run run_study(plan, gate_handler=auto_approve, tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()) to completion N times; observe N orphaned files matching $TMPDIR/tmp*_provenance.json that are never cleaned. Each run grows temp-dir usage by the serialized trail size; nothing ever removes them (no atexit, no unlink).",
    "suggested_fix": "Wrap the artifact upload in try/finally and unlink the temp file after log_artifact returns (or fails): create the path, write+flush+close, then `try: t.log_artifact(rid, path) finally: os.unlink(path)` with missing_ok semantics. Prefer writing into a tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() context so both the file and dir are cleaned deterministically, mirroring versioning.py's mkstemp+try/finally pattern. Optionally set restrictive perms (0o600) since the file holds provenance payloads."
  },
  {
    "id": "SC-2",
    "severity": "low",
    "file": "scripts/capture_transcripts.py",
    "line": 67,
    "title": "Operator-username PII not redacted from transcripts when home-dir name is short (<4 chars) or appears in dash-delimited project slug",
    "description": "_scrub redacts the operator's username two ways: the path rule `(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\\s\"']+` (requires literal '/Users/[REDACTED]') and _USERNAME_RE derived from Path.home().name. The Claude project-dir slug that DEFAULT_SRC points at is dash-delimited (e.g. '-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc'); the path rule does NOT match it (no slashes), so the only thing masking the username inside slugs, git-author strings, and prose is _USERNAME_RE. But _username_pattern() returns None for any home-dir name shorter than 4 chars (to avoid over-redacting common words). For operators whose login is <4 chars (e.g. 'bob','amy','ci', 'root' is also skipped), the username then leaks verbatim into committed transcripts via the slug and any prose mention — defeating the stated #F-024/#G-007 PII-scrubbing goal for that class of operator. The capture script's own docstring claims credential-shaped secrets AND operator PII are redacted, so this is an undisclosed completeness gap rather than documented honest scope.",
    "poc": "Set HOME so Path.home().name='bob'; create $SRC=.../-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc/<id>.jsonl containing the slug path and an author line 'bob'. Run capture_transcripts (or commit via the pre-commit hook). The committed transcripts/sessions/<id>.jsonl still contains 'bob' and '-Users-[REDACTED]-...' unredacted because _USERNAME_RE is None (len<4) and the '/Users/' path rule never matches the dash slug.",
    "suggested_fix": "Always derive the slug form of the home path (replace os.sep with '-') and redact it explicitly regardless of username length; and lower/remove the len<4 skip in favor of a word-boundary-anchored token redaction so short usernames are still masked inside slugs and author strings without clobbering arbitrary common words. At minimum, document in the docstring + transcripts/README that usernames <4 chars are NOT auto-redacted so reviewers know to scrub before publishing."
  },
  {
    "id": "SC-3",
    "severity": "low",
    "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py",
    "line": 192,
    "title": "Module-global reasoning ThreadPoolExecutor cannot recover after interpreter-level shutdown; saturation guard can wedge if a worker thread dies",
    "description": "The reasoning seam uses a single process-global ThreadPoolExecutor (_pool) created at import and shut down via atexit(wait=False). _submit_bounded increments _inflight before submit and relies on add_done_callback(_release) to decrement it when the future completes. The accounting is carefully guarded for KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit during submit/callback attach, and for pool-shutdown (RuntimeError -> PermanentReasoningError with decrement). However, there is no recovery path if a pool worker THREAD itself dies abnormally without the future ever completing (e.g. a C-level crash in a backend that takes down the worker but not the process, or _pool internal state corruption): the done-callback never fires, _inflight is never decremented, and after _POOL_WORKERS such events every subsequent call raises RateLimitError('pool saturated') permanently — bricking all reasoning for the process lifetime with no self-heal (the checkpoint pool, by contrast, is explicitly self-healing). This is a latent availability/leak risk on the exception path that the in-flight accounting does not cover; future.result() timeouts are handled (slot intentionally held), but a vanished future is not.",
    "poc": "Simulate: monkeypatch _pool.submit to return a Future that is never resolved (worker 'died'); call _with_timeout 8 times -> each times out (ReasoningError) but the futures never complete, so _release never runs. The 9th _submit_bounded raises RateLimitError unconditionally and stays that way forever; is_available()/run() for BULK/HIGH_STAKES are effectively dead with no recovery short of process restart.",
    "suggested_fix": "Add a self-heal/watchdog: track each in-flight future with its submission time and, in _submit_bounded's saturation branch, reap futures that are done-but-unreleased or that have exceeded a hard ceiling (per-call timeout + grace) by force-calling _release on them before refusing. Alternatively decrement _inflight in a finally around future.result() in _with_timeout for the timeout case combined with a per-future released flag (already present) so a vanished worker cannot permanently consume a slot. Document that wait=False atexit means in-flight backend calls may be abandoned at exit."
  },
  {
    "id": "R-1",
    "severity": "medium",
    "file": "src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py",
    "line": 512,
    "title": "Malformed reasoning params leak a raw ValueError/TypeError through the typed public boundary (run/run_json)",
    "description": "ReasoningSeam.run / run_json are documented as the single hardened abstraction over LLM execution, returning typed errors (ReasoningError / PermanentReasoningError / RateLimitError) so callers can classify transient vs permanent failures and so _run_with_retries can decide whether to retry. However _attempt() coerces caller-supplied request.params inline BEFORE entering any tier backend: `temperature = float(p.get('temperature', 0.0))` (line 512), `seed = p.get('_seed')` then `int(seed)` (lines 515/527), and `json_mode = bool(...)`. If a caller passes a non-numeric temperature ('hot'), a None temperature, or a non-integer _seed, `float()`/`int()` raises a bare ValueError/TypeError. That exception is raised inside _attempt, which runs under _run_with_retries — whose except clauses catch ONLY PermanentReasoningError/RateLimitError/ReasoningError. A raw ValueError/TypeError therefore propagates straight out of run()/run_json() unwrapped, violating the module's typed-error contract (lines 1-26, 641-647). A caller doing `try: seam.run(req) except ReasoningError:` will not catch it and will crash. The error is also semantically permanent (a malformed param is not retryable), so it should be a PermanentReasoningError, not allowed to escape untyped.",

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

name: Tests

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    name: Phase 0 test suite
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    # Provision Postgres so the checkpointer durability/cross-boundary recovery
    # tests run unconditionally in CI instead of silently skipping — that invariant
    # is exactly where regressions slip in (#260). The tests fail (not skip) in CI
    # if this service is unreachable; see test_postgres_checkpointer.py.
    # A SQLite-saver proxy (langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite, the issue's original
    # proposal) was viable but rejected: exercising the REAL pooled PostgresSaver
    # path here — self-healing pool, advisory-locked migration — covers the actual
    # production durability contract, not a different store's semantics.
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16
        env:
          POSTGRES_USER: postgres
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
          POSTGRES_DB: hcgrc
        ports:
          - 5432:5432
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 10s
          --health-timeout 5s
          --health-retries 5

    env:
      HCGRC_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/hcgrc

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python 3.11
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"
          cache: "pip"
          cache-dependency-path: requirements-ci.txt

      - name: Install CI dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements-ci.txt

      - name: Run the test suite
        # Run ALL tests, not just tests/test_phase0/ — the reasoning_client,
        # observability, and infrastructure suites were never gated by CI (pass-7).
        # Backend-dependent tests (claude-agent-sdk, phoenix.otel, mlflow) are
        # importorskip-guarded so they no-op in the lean CI env; Postgres is provided
        # by the service container above.
        run: python -m pytest tests/ -v --tb=short

1	"""Confirmatory-test machinery (METHODOLOGY.md §2, §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test takes a *locked* hypothesis and returns a :class:`Verdict`. Two
4	mechanisms, selected by hypothesis kind:
5	
6	- unit-level → evaluate on a held-out confirmation partition (Firewall B via
7	  :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`);
8	- whole-corpus → compare the observed statistic against a null/permutation distribution and
9	  require stability across resamples.
10	
11	This module defines the *contract and guards* around confirmation. The concrete statistics
12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, validate the supplied
14	statistics, and map an outcome onto one of the three verdicts honestly (including
15	``indeterminate``).
16	
17	Direction handling (audit pass 1, issue #2): a claim predicts the statistic lies in a
18	particular tail of the null. The empirical p-value is computed against *that* tail, so a
19	claim predicting a low value is confirmable, and a genuine contradiction (significant in the
20	opposite tail) is detectable — both were impossible under the previous direction-blind
21	upper-tail-only implementation.
22	"""
23	
24	from __future__ import annotations
25	
26	import datetime as _dt
27	import math
28	import warnings
29	from typing import Iterable, Sequence
30	
31	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
32	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Direction, Hypothesis, HypothesisKind
33	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
34	    TimestampAuthority,
35	    require_preregistered,
36	)
37	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
38	
39	
40	def _gate_preregistration(hypothesis: Hypothesis, authority: TimestampAuthority | None) -> None:
41	    """Pre-registration gate for the confirm primitives.
42	
43	    If ``authority`` is supplied, run the full :func:`require_preregistered` check (content
44	    binding + verifiable timestamp + lock-before-this-moment) — so a study calling a confirm
45	    primitive *directly* (outside a runner) gets the same guarantee the runners enforce. If it
46	    is ``None``, fall back to the cheap presence gate (:func:`require_locked`); the caller has
47	    then opted out of real verification and owns that choice.
48	    """
49	    if authority is not None:
50	        require_preregistered(
51	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
52	        )
53	    else:
54	        # Falling back to the cheap presence gate means a hand-set timestamp_proof string passes
55	        # WITHOUT any cryptographic verification. Warn so a direct caller cannot silently skip real
56	        # pre-registration (#G-006); the runners always supply an authority and never hit this.
57	        warnings.warn(
58	            "confirm primitive called with authority=None: pre-registration is verified by a "
59	            "PRESENCE check only (lock fields populated), NOT cryptographically. Pass a "
60	            "TimestampAuthority to verify the proof binds the hypothesis and precedes confirmation.",
61	            stacklevel=3,
62	        )
63	        require_locked(hypothesis)
64	
65	
66	def require_locked(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> None:
67	    """Cheap **presence** gate: refuse a hypothesis that carries no lock fields at all.
68	
69	    This is defense-in-depth, not the methodology-grade check. It does not verify the proof
70	    binds the content or that the lock precedes confirmation — that is
71	    :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.require_preregistered`, which the
72	    confirmatory *runners* (:mod:`adjudication`, :mod:`discovery`) call before they confirm.
73	    A study driving :func:`confirm_whole_corpus`/:func:`confirm_unit_level` directly (outside a
74	    runner) should call ``require_preregistered`` itself to get the real guarantee.
75	    """
76	    if not hypothesis.locked:
77	        raise FirewallViolation(
78	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is not locked; confirm is forbidden "
79	            "before pre-registration (Firewall B / pre-registration)"
80	        )
81	
82	
83	def _validate_alpha(alpha: float) -> None:
84	    # A significance threshold of 0.5 or more is not a meaningful one-sided level, and
85	    # admitting it lets both tails register "significant" at once (alpha < 0.5 guarantees
86	    # p_support + p_contra >= 1 cannot both fall below alpha) — fix-review nit on issue #2.
87	    if not (0.0 < alpha < 0.5):
88	        raise ValueError(f"alpha must be in (0, 0.5); got {alpha}")
89	
90	
91	def _validate_pvalue(p_value: float) -> None:
92	    if not (0.0 <= p_value <= 1.0):
93	        raise ValueError(f"p_value must be in [0, 1]; got {p_value}")
94	
95	
96	def _validate_finite(name: str, value: float) -> None:
97	    if not math.isfinite(value):
98	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be a finite number; got {value}")
99	
100	
101	def verdict_from_pvalue(
102	    hypothesis_id: str,
103	    *,
104	    statistic: float,
105	    p_value: float,
106	    alpha: float,
107	    powered: bool,
108	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
109	    test_description: str = "confirmatory test",
110	) -> Verdict:
111	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
112	
113	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
114	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
115	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
116	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here).
117	
118	    ``p_value`` and ``direction_supports_claim`` must be derived by the caller from a test
119	    appropriate to the claim's predicted direction. For a unit-level held-out test the engine
120	    is handed only an opaque ``statistic`` + ``p_value`` and cannot itself infer whether the
121	    statistic supports or contradicts the claim — so ``direction_supports_claim`` is a trusted
122	    caller obligation, ``statistic`` is recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the
123	    verdict), and the deciding flag is recorded in ``evidence`` so the verdict is re-derivable
124	    (audit pass 2, issue #20). Inputs are validated (issue #4) and the recorded decision rule
125	    reflects the caller's ``test_description``, not a hard-coded sidedness label (issue #2).
126	    """
127	    _validate_pvalue(p_value)
128	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
129	    _validate_finite("statistic", statistic)
130	
131	    rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
132	    common = dict(
133	        statistic=statistic,
134	        threshold=alpha,
135	        evidence={"p_value": p_value, "direction_supports_claim": direction_supports_claim},
136	    )
137	    if not powered:
138	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
139	    if p_value <= alpha:
140	        if direction_supports_claim:
141	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
142	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
143	    return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common)
144	
145	
146	def confirm_unit_level(
147	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
148	    *,
149	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
150	    evaluated_ids: Iterable[str],
151	    statistic: float,
152	    p_value: float,
153	    alpha: float,
154	    powered: bool,
155	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
156	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
157	) -> Verdict:
158	    """Confirm a unit-level hypothesis on the held-out partition only.
159	
160	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
161	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
162	
163	    Direction (pass 4, #G-017): unlike :func:`confirm_whole_corpus` — where the verdict polarity
164	    is derived from the pre-registered ``predicted_direction`` tail — the unit-level verdict is
165	    governed SOLELY by the caller-supplied ``direction_supports_claim`` flag (the engine sees only
166	    an opaque held-out ``statistic``/``p_value`` and cannot itself infer the tail). The
167	    hypothesis's ``predicted_direction`` is therefore advisory at unit level: it is shape-validated
168	    below for safety but does NOT decide the verdict. A study that wants the locked tail to bind the
169	    unit-level decision must derive ``direction_supports_claim`` from it in its own confirmer.
170	    """
171	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
172	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
173	    # Defense-in-depth direction validation (pass 3, #F-002): the unit-level path takes the
174	    # deciding direction as a trusted caller flag (``direction_supports_claim``) and so never
175	    # routed through ``_resolve_direction`` — which left a locked-but-invalid
176	    # ``predicted_direction`` (e.g. "UP") entirely unchecked here. ``Hypothesis.__post_init__``
177	    # now rejects such values at construction, but re-validate any pre-registered direction here
178	    # so a hypothesis constructed by an unusual path (or a future field mutation) cannot confirm
179	    # against an unrecognized tail.
180	    if hypothesis.predicted_direction is not None and (
181	        hypothesis.predicted_direction not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS
182	    ):
183	        raise ValueError(
184	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got "
185	            f"{hypothesis.predicted_direction!r}"
186	        )
187	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
188	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
189	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
190	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
191	        statistic=statistic,
192	        p_value=p_value,
193	        alpha=alpha,
194	        powered=powered,
195	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
196	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
197	    )
198	
199	
200	def _empirical_p(null: Sequence[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
201	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
202	    n = len(null)
203	    if tail == "greater":
204	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
205	    else:
206	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
207	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
208	
209	
210	def _resample_stability(
211	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
212	) -> float:
213	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
214	
215	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
216	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
217	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
218	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
219	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
220	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
221	
222	    The default path is O(R·N) pure Python (one null pass per resample). When numpy is available
223	    (the optional ``baseline`` extra) the whole R×N comparison runs as a single vectorized op
224	    (pass 3, #F-017). The numpy path computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts and the same
225	    ``(extreme + 1) / (n + 1) <= alpha`` test, so its result is exactly equal to the Python path;
226	    it is purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change.
227	    """
228	    n = len(null)
229	    try:
230	        import numpy as np
231	
232	        # Use searchsorted on a sorted null (O((R+N) log N) time, O(R) memory) rather than the
233	        # R×N broadcast boolean matrix (#G-010): the matrix peaks at ~R*N bytes (250MB for
234	        # R=5000,N=50000) and can OOM where the pure-Python loop would not. The integer
235	        # extreme-counts are IDENTICAL to _empirical_p: for 'greater', count(null >= r) = N -
236	        # searchsorted(sorted, r, 'left'); for 'less', count(null <= r) = searchsorted(sorted, r,
237	        # 'right'). Then (extreme+1)/(n+1) <= alpha, same as the pure path.
238	        sorted_null = np.sort(np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64))
239	        res_arr = np.asarray(resamples, dtype=np.float64)

306	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).

309	    (issue #24). The pattern must (a) beat the null at ``alpha`` in the predicted tail and

311	    not asserted by the caller (audit pass 2, issue #21). Outcomes:

314	    - significant in the *opposite* tail → ``contradicted``;

336	    p_support = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, direction)

345	        for r in resample_statistics:

347	        stability = _resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)
348	        # Also measure how consistently the resamples reproduce the OPPOSITE-tail significance, so a
349	        # contradiction's robustness is recorded in evidence even though (by design) a contradiction
350	        # is reportable without it (#G-005).

369	    }

375	    # evidence OF the opposite — not absence of evidence — so it stands on the observed effect.
